narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your outstanding undergraduate GPA of 3.80, which stands well above the typical student's 3.21. However, the admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 59.7% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejecting your application to prioritize those with lower predicted risks. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far below the average of around 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school raised concerns about bar readiness compared to most applicants. This was closely balanced by your strong undergrad GPA, which powerfully pushed the prediction in the favorable direction toward passing the bar, much like it does for top performers in the dataset. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction, reinforcing worries about early academic standing relative to peers. Other elements, like your LSAT score of 28—below the usual 36.7—nudged it slightly toward failure, while your middle family income bracket had minimal sway, and your full-time student status offered a small positive boost, aligning with the vast majority of successful applicants. Overall, your profile contrasts with typical admitted students who tend to have stronger school deciles alongside high test scores, even if your GPA shines brightly. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a brilliant Black woman ready to break barriers in law—it's the model's data-driven call based on bar passage patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking ways to strengthen your school performance deciles through additional coursework or programs, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience and undergrad excellence. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh your unique background more heavily. Keep pushing; many have turned similar predictions around and thrived. We're rooting for you.
